text: North Korea restarted its nuclear program roughly after the US had invaded Iraq, because they knew they could be next. I'm not saying that it's great North Korea had developed its nukes, but at the same time they did it for good reason. The Americans can blame their own disastrous foreign policy for that. Now, there is a reason why nuclear-armed states do not fight each other, and if there is a potential for conflict, they prefer to talk. That's because the alternative is much worse and there can be no winners. North Koreans know that too, and I doubt they'd be willing to start a war they can't win. Therefore, it's naive to claim that they will become "immune to diplomacy". Of all things, diplomacy becomes the only thing available to influence policy when the alternative is nuclear weapons.

text:
Governments will always be predisposed towards tax-and-spend.
It takes either an extreme financial/fiscal crisis, or a government with a strong sense of ideology/discipline to prod governments towards the straight and narrow path of fiscal prudence. Not to mention how hard it is to root out entrenched interests (public employee payrolls are a bulging low-hanging fruit, but good luck to those who try to prune it).

Even our last Conservative government (despite a good start) was not immune.
And the Liberal governments of the 90s adopted fiscal conservatism out of necessity.

For those of us who question the efficacy of government efforts/spending to spur the economy, or the benefits of heavy-handed government intervention into individuals' lives and the market, the only logical solution is to put more power into the hands of the individual citizen by shrinking government. text:
Another thoughtful article. So many concepts in a short piece. Books are a free airplane ticket to the elsewhere; they provide escape to other worlds. You mention Alaska's horrible track record with kids and I'm thinking this doesn't have to be this way; we are in the bad category in many other areas as well, and the rest can change, too.

The adverse experiences you mentioned would probably be great fodder for another piece. (ACES) adverse childhood experiences have long- term medical implications; if for no other reason than finances, we need to pay attention to these as the social costs of ignoring these is tremendous.

Reading can mend and facilitate survival. In this medium one finds many perspectives that aid life. Tablet computers can be a great way to improve the quality of a person's life-unless they forsake exercise in order to satisfy some computer addiction:)

I agree there are other ways to handle this. 
I can also see some good and pitfalls falling along this. 
On the good it's taking a nice step towards better health care and hopefully less crappy profit driven insurance and health consultants (for lack of more appropriate term)
On the bad, they are looking too short sighted and a little too exclusive.
There are local companies who could probably do a great job on this project. There are probably other industries that would be interested in investing in this to be included. 
This may also be stepping on the toes of history here. Don't forget the companies that owned and dictated where their employees lived, and that went horribly. (Can't really remember much specific about it, mid 1800s I think)
label: non-biased
